scriptId: cs_v3_ACCUMULATION_L40_Medium-term plans for the next year
historyId: h_v3_ACCUMULATION_L40_Medium-term plans for the next year

goal: Balance feature work, reliability improvements, and growth experiments in a one-year plan.
Incorporate retention and onboarding improvements with modest targets and limited promo spend.
constraints: Quarters organized by a theme with three pillars: Features, Reliability & Foundation, Experiments & Growth.
Capacity guardrails: minimum 20–30% reliability capacity; 10–20% experiments capacity; remaining for features with one flex slot.
Timeboxing experiments to 2–8 weeks; stop/scale criteria predefined.
Monthly metric review; quarterly roadmap reprioritization; defined owners and success criteria.
Limit WIP and concurrent epics: allow each core team 1 major epic + 1 small project to avoid context switching.
Use a score cutoff: only initiatives above X score proceed into planning; everything else goes into backlog.
Require an owner and a success metric before work starts — no work without a measurable outcome.
Thin-slice scope: prefer smaller MVPs that can be shipped in one cycle; defer polish.
Batch related work: group dependent tasks together to reduce coordination overhead.
Checkpoints must be defined before work starts and documented using a reusable checkpoint template.
Each checkpoint requires owner, primary metric, clear thresholds for Continue / Pivot / Stop, timeboxed measurement window, and required evidence.
Governance cadence includes locking checkpoints into the quarterly plan and calendar invites.
Require a 1-pager with the checkpoint template filled in 48 hours before each checkpoint meeting.
Log decisions and next steps in the roadmap tool.
Budget cap: $500k per cycle to cover development, infrastructure changes, and contractors; require explicit capacity mapping, contingency (10–20%), and checkpoints before major spend.
Lead engineer leave: late spring for ~3 months; adjust capacity planning to account for reduced core capacity.
Develop backups and explicit owner assignments for initiatives led by the departing engineer; ensure overlap during handoffs.
Consider short-term contractor support to cover critical path work during the leave; fund from contingency if needed.
Update roadmap and checkpoint dates to reflect leave window; flag impacted milestones for fast decision-making.
Preserve reliability by maintaining maintenance buffer during the leave period.
Contractor onboarding and knowledge-transfer requirements: explicit overlap, ramp time, mandatory handoff documentation and deliverables.
Discovery phase (paid) to refine scope before contractor work.
Hard deadline: custom integration must be delivered by end of Q2; non-optional for client rollout.
decisions: Adopt a quarterly plan structure with a Theme and three Pillars (Features, Reliability & Foundation, Experiments & Growth).
Apply capacity guardrails per quarter; Reliability 20–30%, Experiments 10–20%, Features remainder with one flexible slot.
Allocate 1–2 features, 1–2 reliability projects, and 2 experiments per quarter (adjustable per capacity).
Define stop/scale criteria for experiments (e.g., >10% uplift scale; <3% uplift after N users stop).
KPIs: assign one KPI per pillar (Feature, Reliability, Experiments).
Governance cadence: monthly metric reviews; quarterly roadmap reprioritization; clearly assign owners and success criteria.
Prioritization methods: RICE
Prioritization methods: WSJF
Prioritization methods: ICE
Prioritization methods: Value vs Effort
Prioritization methods: Kano / Opportunity
Prioritization methods: Technical debt triage
Adopt a formal checkpoint governance: lock checkpoints into quarterly plan and calendar invites; require a 1-page 48 hours before checkpoint.
Standardize on a reusable checkpoint template for all initiatives (features, reliability work, experiments).
Make binary continue/pivot/stop decisions with documented rationale; assign decision authority and deadline.
Adopt a $500k cycle budget cap with allocations to development, infra, and contractors; require contingency runway and predefined checkpoints; map dollars to capacity.
Introduce a contractor vs hire payoff framework to guide bridging gaps; evaluate duration, skill specificity, urgency/cost of delay, onboarding overhead, long-term ownership, and budget constraints.
Contractor engagements should use fixed-price or time-and-material with a not-to-exceed cap and defined acceptance criteria.
Enforce mandatory overlap and knowledge-transfer: required overlap with internal staff, documented deliverables, tests, and runbooks.
Include paid discovery to refine scope before contractor work.
Include a post-handoff warranty period for bug fixes after transfer.

Signals to track for quarterly big bets: Primary outcome metric (north star) and its measurement window.
Leading indicators: upstream funnel metrics that predict the primary outcome.
Adoption & usage: percent of target cohort using the new feature, frequency of use, and depth of usage.
Retention & cohort behavior: retention curves and cohort analysis (e.g., day 30, day 90) for exposed groups.
Business impact / monetization signals: revenue, conversion to paid, ARPU, churn on paying users.
Quality & reliability guardrails: error rate, incident count, SLA breaches, MTTR, and support volume.
Operational & cost signals: engineering velocity impact, deployment failures, and infrastructure cost overruns.
Statistical & confidence signals: p-values, confidence intervals, minimum detectable effect, and required sample size.
Qualitative signals: user interviews, NPS/comments, support feedback themes.
Strategic & pipeline signals: inbound enterprise interest, partner requests, and alignment with executive priorities.
Cadence & decision rules: monitoring cadence (real-time to quarterly), and the continue/pivot/stop criteria at checkpoints.

Pitfalls and mitigations for translating ambitions into a 12-month roadmap: common issues and mitigations including overcommitment, treating roadmap as tasks, neglecting maintenance, prioritization issues, missing measurable success criteria, lack of quarter milestones, ignoring dependencies, single points of failure, no contingency, not timeboxing experiments, focus on outputs not impact, weak governance, staffing volatility, external constraints, and mitigations such as capacity planning discipline, outcome-focused initiatives, explicit checkpoints, and governance.
Budget cap: $500k per cycle to cover development, infrastructure changes, and contractors; require explicit capacity mapping, contingency (10–20%), and checkpoints before major spend.
Client integration deadline: end of Q2; hard constraint for client rollout.
open: Select an allocation template (Balanced, Reliability-first, Growth-first).
Define initial quarterly themes for Q1 through Q4.
Populate quarterly plans with specific initiatives: 1–2 features, 1 reliability project, and 2 experiments per quarter.
Document prioritization methods and scorecutoffs to guide planning.
Budget-to-capacity mapping: convert $500k into capacity and map to top-priority initiatives.
Integration milestones and acceptance criteria to meet end-of-Q2 deadline.
Provide a short one-pager template to document the client integration plan and a recommended capacity split for the next two quarters.
Define retention baselines and targets (day-7/day-30) with MDE and measurement windows for retention experiments.
Specify leading indicators for retention (onboarding completion, time-to-value) and plan experiments accordingly.
